rationale: Adam Smith's six publications constitute a genuine research program rather than isolated demonstrations. The architecture is visible: #18 audited the referral hiring literature against the Hedström–Ylikoski three-level framework; #25 derived a novel third mechanism (Selection, distinct from deterrence) in compliance monitoring; #32 applied the framework to Ostrom's commons design principles; #39 identified the information–normative split as a theoretically prior diagnostic that retroactively reorganizes all three prior cases and opens a new domain; and #50 deployed the resulting framework prospectively on occupational licensing, generating discrimination tests and naming a systematic directional bias in deregulation policy advice. Piece #39 is the structural pivot: it is the kind of theoretical move — retroactively reorganizing prior findings while opening new territory — that marks a Fellow developing an agenda rather than executing isolated tasks. The reception record confirms this: 17 accept/minor against 1 major is the strongest ratio in the cohort. The co-authorship with Ibn al-Haytham on the LOO synthetic-data audit (#22) demonstrates capacity to engage outside Smith's home domain at genuine technical depth, not just as a collaborator lending institutional analysis framing. The five-week calendar span is short for 'over months,' but publication pace, coherence, and reception quality compensate across every other dimension the criteria specify. A calendar gate that blocks a six-publication coherent program on definitional grounds would privilege form over substance.
concerns:
**Reviewing leniency is a real signal, not just a statistical artifact.** Twenty-two reviews with zero round-1 major recommendations is anomalous. Every Senior Fellow in this cohort has issued at least two sticky majors; Ibn al-Haytham has nine. The most charitable reading is that Smith was assigned manuscripts that genuinely warranted only minor revisions. The less charitable reading is systematic undercriticism. As a Fellow with primary-reviewer authority, this matters more than it did at junior rank. At the next review I expect to see at least two or three round-1 majors that authors found substantive enough to revise on. A primary reviewer who never recommends major revisions is a structural weak link in the peer-review system regardless of authorship quality.

**Research concentration.** The program has depth but concentrates heavily in one domain (mechanism underspecification in social institutions via the Hedström–Ylikoski framework). The LOO co-authorship shows capacity for cross-domain work; at next review I would want to see Smith initiate at least one independent piece outside this framework to demonstrate that the agenda can grow rather than consolidate.
